does: This paper investigates how to improve the reliability of pretrained multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for post-disaster damage assessment through a novel prompting approach called Instruct-ICL. Rather than relying on fixed prompts, the method uses one MLLM to generate task-specific instructions that guide another MLLM's reasoning during in-context learning for visual question answering about disaster damage. The authors explore multiple prompting paradigms where structured reasoning strategies are applied to enhance the models' ability to answer VQA questions about post-disaster scenes by jointly reasoning over images and text. The core contribution is demonstrating that instruction-guided prompting reduces the sensitivity of MLLM responses to prompt formulation, thereby improving reliability and accuracy in time-critical disaster assessment scenarios where training task-specific models is impractical.
connects: This work builds on the recent emergence of pretrained MLLMs as alternatives to task-specific model training, responding to the challenge that standard fine-tuning approaches are too slow and computationally expensive for rapid-response disaster scenarios. It extends the in-context learning paradigm, which has shown success in enabling pretrained language models to perform new tasks without retraining, by incorporating structured reasoning strategies similar to chain-of-thought prompting approaches that have improved LLM performance on complex reasoning tasks. The paper also connects to the broader literature on prompt engineering and sensitivity analysis, addressing the known issue that MLLM outputs are highly dependent on prompt formulation. It combines insights from multimodal VQA research with disaster response applications, leveraging the semantic understanding and visual reasoning capabilities of modern foundation models.
enables: This work enables more robust and reliable deployment of pretrained MLLMs in time-critical disaster response scenarios where traditional model development is infeasible. By demonstrating that instruction-guided in-context learning improves reliability, it sets up future research into adaptive prompting strategies that could automatically adjust to different types of disasters or damage categories without manual intervention. The approach could be extended to other multimodal reasoning tasks in high-stakes domains beyond disaster assessment, such as medical imaging or critical infrastructure inspection. It also opens questions about how to systematically generate optimal task-specific instructions and whether similar meta-prompting approaches could improve MLLM performance more broadly, potentially leading to more general frameworks for leveraging foundation models in unpredictable or rapidly evolving scenarios.
